State of California

Memorandum

M- 82-163

DATE: September 24, 1982

TO: Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger

FROM: Fair Political/Practices Commission
Barbara Milman/[illegible] Fishburn, Bob Leigbh

RE: Summary of Research on Legislative Immunity from Civil
Process and in Civil Proceedings

I. Constitutional Immunity of Legislators from Civil
Process.

A. Cal. Const. Art. IV, Section 14:

A member of the legislature is not subject to civil
process during a session of the legislature or for
five days before and after a session. (Added Nov. 8,
1966)

B. The only case interpreting the present section is
Harmer v. Superior Court, 275 Cal. App. 3d 345 (1969),
which held:

(1) Art. IV, Section 14 creates an exemption from
civil process without qualification as to kind or
subject matter of the lawsuit.

(2) This immunity is designed to benefit the
public by protecting legislators against compelled
distraction and interference during the legislative
session.

(3) The immunity applies to civil process
generally.

(4) There are conceivable situations where this
immunity might amount to a denial of due process, but
this is not one of them.

Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger
September 24, 1982
Page 2


NOTE:  A state agency does not have the federal or
state due process rights of an individual.  California
Employment Stabilization Comm'n. v. Payne (1947) 31
C.2d 210, 215.

C. Definitions of "Civil Process"

     (1)  Statutory Definitions

          (a)  Government Code Section 22 - "Process"
includes a writ or summons issued in the course of
judicial proceedings of either a civil or criminal
nature.

          (b)  Government Code Section 26660 - Title 3,
Government of Counties:

               As used in this title:

               (a)  "Process" includes all writs, warrants,
summons, and order of courts of justice, or
judicial officers.

               (b)  "Notice" includes all papers and orders
required to be served in any proceedings before
any court, board, or officer, or when required
by law to be served independently of such
proceeding.

               (c)  Code of Civil Procedure Section 17

                    6.  ... and the word "process" a writ or
summons issued in the course of judicial
proceedings;

     (2)  Case Law

          (a)  "Process is a means whereby a court compels
a compliance with its demands."  Woodcourt II
Limited v. McDonald Company, 119, Cal. App. 3d 245
(1981); Lister v. Superior Court, 98 Cal. App. 3d
64 (1979); Labrovich v. Georgision, 144 Cal. App.
2d 567, 574 (1976), 50 Cal. Jur. 3d., "Process,
Notice and Subpoenas," Section 1, P. 638.  Service
of a summons is civil process.  Long v. Ansell,
(1934) 69 F2d 386, ___.

Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger
September 24, 1982
Page 3


     (b) The definition of "process" has never been
extended to a proceeding that in no wise emanates
from or rests upon the authority or jurisdiction of
a court. Meadows v. Bakersfield S. & L. Ass'n.,
259 Cal. App. 2d 749, 753 (1967). (Posting notice
of default and provisions of a deed of trust is not
"process.") Caminetti v. Guarantee Union Life Ins.
Co., 22 Cal. 2d 750, 763 (1943). (Supersedeas will
not issue to prevent Ins. Comm'r. from enforcing a
court-approved agreement; supersedeas will issue
only to restrain court below or its officers from
proceeding to enforce a judgment, and it will not
issue where "no process of or action by the court
below is involved.")

     (c) A subpoena issued by a court clerk or a
district attorney is not "legal process." The
issuance of such a subpoena is a "purely
ministerial" act, which cannot be enforced until a
judicial determination of its legality has been
made. People v. Blair (1979) 25 Cal. 3d 640, 648
(fn 6), 651.

     (d) However, the immunity against "civil
process" has been read broadly by some state
courts. A subpoena duces tecum for a pretrial
discovery deposition in a judicial proceeding was
held to be "civil process" for purposes of a
legislator's constitutional privilege. Bishop v.
Wayne Circuit Judge (1976) 395 Mich. 672, 676. So
was a post-judgment writ of garnishment. In
rejecting the argument that the garnishment, served
directly          on the state, was not civil
process, the court said (quoting an earlier case):

     "This is to narrow view of the situation. The
idea back of the constitutional provision was to
protect the legislators from the trouble, worry
and inconvenience of court proceedings during
the session, and for a certain time before and
after, so that the State could have their
undivided time and attention in public affairs.
[the legislator], as principal defendant, had
the right to make a defense to the garnishee
proceeding. In the present case the garnishee
proceeding succeeded in doing just what the

Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger
September 24, 1982
Page 4


                    constitutional provision was created to avoid.
                    It harassed the legislator, drove him to make a
                    defense in the garnishee proceeding, and
                    deprived him of the means of subsistence pending
                    the balance of the session. We think the case
                    clearly comes within the constitutional
                    inhibition." 234 Mich 541-542.

                              Bishop v. Wayne Circuit Judge
                              (1976) 395 Mich. 672, 675-676

     The Wisconsin Attorney General has also ruled
that a quasi-garnishment proceeding (which does not
involve the courts, but only requires a filing with
the Secretary of State) is civil process for
purposes of a state constitutional privilege. The
Attorney General argued that such a broad
interpretation was necessary to carry out the
purposes of the privilege. Opinions of the
Attorney General of the State of Wisconsin, Vol.
XX, pp. 29-31 (1931). See also Auditor General v.
Wayne Circuit Judge (1926) 234 Mich. 540, 208 NW
696.

     NOTE: A writ of post-judgment garnishment can be
issued only after a judicial determination of the
merits of a case.

     (3) Is "administrative process" the same as "civil
process"?

     Administrative agencies are not courts of justice
within the meaning of the Code of Civil Procedure, and
administrative agency officials are not officers of
the court since they derive their power from statute
and are subject to judicial supervision only to
prevent arbitrary excercises of power. Swars v.
Vallejo, 33 Cal. 2d 867 (19 ); Caminetti v. Guarantee
Union Life Ins. Co., supra, at p. 763. Cf., Velkov v.
Superior Court, 40 Cal. 2d 289 (1953) (State Bar
disciplinary proceedings are a function of the Supreme
Court, and State Bar operates as intermediary agency
in making findings and recommendations). Like a
prosecutor's or clerk's subpoena, an administrative
subpoena can only be enforced after a judicial

Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger
September 24, 1982
Page 5


determination that it is valid. Government Code
Sections 11525, 11180, et seq. See People v. Blair,
supra. The same is true of other administrative
orders. Section 11525. Thus an administrative
agency's orders and subpoenas should not constitute
"civil process."

D. Definition of a "session" of the Legislature:

     (1) Cal. Const., Art. IV, Section 3.

          (a) The Legislature shall convene in regular
     session ... on the first Monday in December of each
     even-numbered year .... Each session ... shall
     adjourn sine die by operation of the Constitution
     ... November 30 of the following even-numbered
     year.

          (b) On extraordinary occasions the Governor by
     proclamation may cause the Legislature to assemble
     in special session....

          (c) There is no case law in California
     interpreting the term "session."

     (2) Other jurisdictions:

          (a) When legislators have a constitutional
     immunity during a legislative "session," courts
     have tended to interpret the word "session"
     broadly.

          1. A grant of immunity "during the session"
     and for fifteen days before the commencement of
     the session should be "broadly construed," since
     the purpose is "to protect the legislators from
     distraction during the stated periods of time."
     Semans v. Walgren (1973) 82 Wash. 2d 771, 514 P.
     2d 166, 168.

          2. During an "intra-session adjournment" or
     "recess" (July 13-Sept. 17), which is not an
     adjournment sine die, the privilege from civil
     process during the "session" applies. Bishop v.
     Wayne Circuit Judge (1976) 395 Mich. 672,
     675-676.

Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger
September 24, 1982
Page 6


3.  A "special session" is also a "session"
within the constitutional immunity.  Bishop v.
Wayne Circuit Judge, supra; Opinions of the
Attorney General of Wisc., Vol XX, 1931, Opinion
requested by T. Dammann, p. 529, 530.

4.  The United States Supreme Court has also
suggested a liberal construction of the term
session in a case involving imposition of a
criminal sentence during a congressional
adjournment:

... if ... the privilege ... extends to an
arrest for any criminal offense, such
privilege would embrace exemption from any
exertion of power by way of arrest and
prosecution for the commission of crime, the
effect of which ... would be to prevent a
congressman from attending a future as well
as a pending session of Congress.

Williamson v. US (1908) 207 US
425, 434 28 SCt 163, 165.

(3)  "Continuous" sessions:

None of the cases from other jurisdictions
involved continuous legislative sessions.  There is
a suggestion that courts would interpret
legislative immunity differently in the case of a
continuous session.

A question is raised by respondent about the
possible application of this rule if a
continuing legislative session is created by
law.  We do not decide that question at this
time.  However, it has been observed in Harmer
v. Superior Court, supra, 275 Cal.App.2d at page
349, 69 Cal.Rptr. at page 857, that "Laws
creating an immunity from judicial process,
however temporary, inevitably trench upon the
judicial function, hence may encounter
constitutional objections."  Thurmond v.
Superior Court, 66 Cal.2d 836, 839-840, 59 Cal.

Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger
September 24, 1982
Page 7


                    Rptr. 273, 427 P.2d 985 (1967); Granai v.
                    Witters, Longmoore, Akley & Brown, 123 Vt. 468,
                    194 A.2d 391 (1963).

                                        Seamans v. Walgren, (1973) 82
                                        Wash. 2d 771, 514 P.2d 166,
                                        168.

        E. History of Art. IV, Section 14

             (1)  Constitution of 1849: Art. IV, Section 12 of
        the California Constitution of 1849 provided:

             Members of the Legislature shall, in all cases
        except treason, felony, and breach of the peace be
        privileged from arrest, and shall not be subject to
        any civil process, during the session of the
        Legislature, nor the fifteen days next before the
        commencement and after the termination of each
        session.

             (2)  Constitution of 1879: In the 1879
        Constitution the section was moved to Article IV,
        Section 11; its text remained unchanged. A comparison
        of the 1879 Constitution with those then in effect in
        other states shows numerous similar provisions in
        existence at the time.

             (3)  1966 Amendment

             In 1966, the California Constitution Revision
        Commission (Feb. 1966) recommended that the section be
        amended as follows:

             Sec. 14. A member of the Legislature is not
        subject to civil process during a session of the
        Legislature or for 5 days before and after a
        session.

             Comment: Provisions purporting to confer immunity
        from arrest to a legislator were deleted as
        surplusage. The courts have determined the
        immunity extends only to civil arrest [In re Emmett
        (1932) 120 Cal. App. 349, 353-357, 7 P.2d 1096], a
        rarely used device.

Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger
September 24, 1982
Page 8

The period of immunity from civil process before
and after legislative sessions was reduced from 15
to 5 days.  Due to modern modes of travel, the
legislator no longer needs 15 days to travel to and
from the legislative seat.

The Comment makes it clear that the understanding
was that this protection covered the actual working
session of the legislature and a legislator's travels
to and from those sessions.  This is consistent with
the interpretation given by other states' courts to
their respective comparable constitutional or
statutory protections afforded to members of their
respective legislative bodies.

This proposal was adopted in 1966; Section 14 has
not been amended since.

(4)  Changes in the sections on legislative
sessions

(a)  When the legislative immunity provision was
first adopted (1849) the Legislature met only for
relatively short "sessions."  The immunity thus did
not in any way prevent lawsuits against
legislators.

(b)  In 1966, the same year as the last change
in Article IV, Section 14, the following additional
proposal was made for two-year legislative sessions
of 166 days each by the California Constitution
Revision Commission and was adopted by the voters:

The Legislature

- Sessions under the existing
Constitution, the Legislature meets during
odd-numbered years in general session at
which all types of legislation can be
considered.  In even-numbered years the
Legislature meets in budget session and the
agenda is generally limited to budget items.

To provide sufficient time to adequately
consider the ever increasing volume of bills
and other matters coming before the
Legislature, the Commission deleted the
restricted budget sessions, and provided, in
proposed Section 4(a), for annual regular
sessions.  Each session will be 166 days in
length - the same as is currently provided
for general sessions (120 days excluding
Saturdays and Sundays).

Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger
September 24, 1982
Page 9

During the two-year term for which
Assemblymen are elected, the Legislature will
be a continuous body. Legislation remaining
at the end of the first session will survive
until the end of the following year's
session....

(c) 1972 Amendment. A new proposal was adopted
in 1972, which changed the sessions from 166 days
per year to continuous sessions. There was no
corresponding change in Article IV, Sec. 14. The
ballot argument in favor of Proposition 4 (November
1972) which created the two-year sessions contained
the following assertions:

The amendment does not mean that the
Legislature will be meeting continuously in
Sacramento for two years. In fact, there are
more limits on the amount of time the
Legislature may act on legislation than
currently exist. These limits are designed to
give the Legislature a more business-like set of
deadlines for its operation.

The amendment will not result in any increase
in compensation or benefits to legislators.

There is no indication that anyone considered
the possible effect of this amendment on Art. IV,
Section 14, or that anyone intended to provide
legislators with immunity from civil process during
virtually their entire term of office.

(d) Present Legislative practice: The
Legislature now convenes on the first Monday in
December of even-numbered years and "adjourns sine
die" on November 30 two years later, it "adjourns"
in "final recess" on the 31st of August in even
numbered years. In addition, there are various
other "adjournments" and recesses which occur
during the course of the "continuous" two-year
session. (Joint Rule 51, Calif. Legis.)

II. Statutory Privilege of Legislators in Judicial and
Administrative "Proceedings."

A. Code of Civil Procedure Section 595 provides:

Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger
September 24, 1982
Page 10


The trial of any civil action, or proceeding in a
court, or of any administrative proceeding before a
state board or commission or officer, irrespective of
the date of the filing thereof or when it became at
issue, or the hearing of any motion, demurrer, or
other proceeding, shall be postponed to a date certain
when it appears to the court, board, commission, or
officer before which such action or proceeding is
pending hast either a party thereto, or any attorney of
record therein (whether he became an attorney of
record before or after the commencement of a
legislative session or before or after his appointment
to a legislative committee), or a principal witness,
is a Member of the Legislature of this state and that
the Legislature is in session or recess (not exceeding
a recess of forty (40) days....

...

When the Legislature is in session or in recess such
action or proceeding shall not, without the consent of
the attorney of record therein, be brought on for
trial or hearing before the expiration of thirty (30)
days next following final adjournment of the
Legislature or the commencement of a recess of more
than forty (40) days. If a date is available during
recess, continuance shall be given if possible to such
earlier date....

...

Granting of a continuance pursuant to this section is
mandatory unless the court determines that such
continuance would defeat or abridge a right to relief
pendente lite in a paternity action or a right to
invoke a provisional remedy such as pendente lite
support in a domestic relations controversy,
attachment and sale of perishable goods, receivership
of a failing business, and temporary restraining order
or preliminary injunction, and that the continuance
should not be granted....

B. Case Law (California)

(1) Thurmond v. Superior Court (9167) 66 Cal.2d
836:

Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger
September 24, 1982
Page 11


          (a)  The Legislature may not enact laws
     concerning continuances which unduly interfere with
     the court's inherent (constitutional) power to
     control its casework and to safeguard the rights of
     litigants.  Any such law will be read as directory
     (not mandatory).  (After Thurmond Section 595 was
     amended to state explicity that it is mandatory.)

          (b)  When a right to relief pendente lite (in
     this case for support for an unborn child) would be
     materially impaired or destroyed by allowing
     continuances under Section 595, the court in its
     discretion should not apply Section 595.  This may
     also apply to other provisional remedies in cases
     involving "substantial existing rights" which could
     be "defeated or abridged by extended continuances"
     Id. at 839-840.

     (2)  Beckley v. Reclamation Bd., 48 Cal. 2d 710
(1957).

     A lawyer-legislator is entitled to continuances
under Section 595 while he is pursuing his
legislative duties; it is an abuse of discretion
for a trial court to not vacate a judgment when
such continuances are not granted.

     (3)  Oil Workers Intern. Union, CIO v. Sup. Ct.,
103 Cal. App. 2d 52 (1951).

     Section 595 does not apply before the Session
begins; it only applies when the Legislature is
actually in Session or in recess during a Session.

     (4)  Peo. v. Goldenson, 76 Cal. 328 (1888).

          (a)  Section 595 applies only to those who are
     attorneys of record before the Session begins.

          (b)  The objectives of Section 595 are to
     protect clients whose attorneys were called to
     their legislative duties and to secure lawyers in
     the Legislature from loss to their private
     business.

Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger
September 24, 1982
Page 12


C. Case Law (other jurisdictions):

     Other states have held unconstitutional statutes
similar to Section 595 which granted legislators a
right to continuances or a privilege against civil
process.

     (1)  A Vermont statute similar to Section 595
granted an attorney-legislator a privilege against
having his cases heard during a legislative session
unless the legislator waived the privilege. The
Vermont court held this unconstitutional because it
violated the state constitution's grant of judicial
powers to the courts (separation of powers), and
because it violated a state constitutional
prohibition against granting "special privileges"
to legislators (or others), Granai v. Witters,
Longmoore, Akley & Brown (1963) A2d 391.

     (2)  An Illinois statute provided that
legislators were immune from civil process during
the session of the legislature. This provision was
held to violate a state constitutional prohibition
(like Vermont's) against any "special laws" which
grant any individual "any special or exclusive
privilege [or] immunity."  "The members of the
General Assembly constitute but a portion of the
public officials of the state, and we perceive no
good reason why they should be singled out as
immune from service of civil process while engaged
in the line of their duty any more than the members
of any other class of public official." Phillips
v. Browne, 270 Ill. 450, 110 N.E. 601, 602 (1915).

     (3)  Other jurisdictions have also held similar
statutes to be an unconstitutional infringement of
judicial powers. Lemoine v. Martineau (R.I. 1975)
342 A2d 616, 621; City v. Valdez Development Co.
(Alas. 1973) 506 P.2d 1279; Williams v. Borden's,
262 SE 2d 881.

III. Other immunities or privileges from judicial process.

     A. Immunity rule for nonresident witnesses and attorneys
was founded on the convenience of the court, and this
privilege from service always ceased when the benefit

Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger
September 24, 1982
Page 13


to the court ended. Gaines v. Superior Court, 196
Cal. App. 2d 749 (1961) (application to nonresident
attorneys); People v. Wilson, 213 Cal. App. 2d 290
(1963); Velkor v. Superior Court, supra.

This rule was essentially abolished in California
by the Court of Appeal and a liberalized service of
process status. Severn v. Adidas Sportschuhfabriken,
33 Cal. App. 3d 754 (1976) (outlines history of
immunity rule). CCP Section 410.10 et seq.; 50 Cal.
Jur. 3d "Process, Notice, and Subpoenas," Section 53
pp. 701-703.

B. Members of the armed forces will not normally be
exempt from civil process under California law.
Murrey v. Murrey, 216 Cal. 707 (1932); Tulley v.
Superior Court, 45 Cal. App. 2d 24 (1941); 50 Cal.
Jur. 3d, "Process, Notice and Subpoenas," Section 55,
p. 704.

In both of these cases, the courts did not grant an
exemption to service. The courts recognized the
public policy behind such an exemption for all
nonresidents within the jurisdiction engaged in the
performance of a public duty, but they rejected its
application under the particular facts they were faced
with. The Tully court noted that the trial court,
upon a proper showing, could grant reasonable
continuances and otherwise protect the defendant's
rights to prepare his case.

C. Foreign diplomatic officials are immune from process
of state courts based on the doctrine of sovereign
immunity from suit. Federal courts have exclusive
jurisdiction of all actions and proceedings against
foreign officials by statute. U.S. Const., Art. III,
Section 2. 22 U.S.C. Section 252 and 28 U.S.C.
Sections 1251 and 1351; 50 Cal. Jur. 3d "Process,
Notice and Subpoenas," Section 56, p. 705.

State courts have jurisdiction over a suit against
a consular representative and citizen of a foreign
country for divorce and alimony since federal courts
have no jurisdiction over divorce. The term "suits
against consuls and vice-consuls" in the statute must

Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger
September 24, 1982
Page 14


                    be taken to refer to ordinary civil proceedings and
                    not divorce proceedings. State of Ohio ex rel.
                    Popovici, 50 S.Ct. 154 (1930).

                    NOTE: None of the above immunities are based on a
                    constitutionally conferred privilege. Accordingly, the
                    discretion and flexibility allowed in the application of
                    these immunities are not necessarily available to the
                    courts in determining the extent of a legislator's
                    immunity under Art. IV, Sec. 14. However, they support
                    the court's ability to interpret Section 595 narrowly,
                    and also provide some support for the argument that the
                    constitutional immunity from civil process should be
                    construed narrowly and literally. See also Long v.
                    Ansell, 55 S.Ct. 21 (1934), where the Supreme Court held
                    that a U.S. Senator's constitutional privilege against
                    civil arrest did not extend to an immunity from civil
                    process. "Its language is exact and leaves no room for a
                    construction which would extend the privilege beyond the
                    terms of the grant." Id., at 22. But see cases cited in
                    Section I C (2)(d), above.

        IV.  Waiver

             A.  Cases

                 (1)  In re Emmett, 120 Cal. App. 349, 359-360
                      (1932). (Involving claim of privilege from arrest
                      under former constitutional provision.)

                      The exemption is a personal privilege, and it is
                      waived if it is not claimed at the "proper time,"
                      which, in a criminal case is by plea or motion before
                      trial. The arrest is valid until the exemption is
                      claimed and granted.

                 (2)  Other cases have held that a legislator's
                      constitutional immunity from arrest can be waived, or
                      the doctrine of estoppel may apply. Kallock v. Elward
                      (1919) 108 Atl. 256, 8ALR 750, 752; Johnson v. Johnson
                      (1785) 4 Call 38 (Virginia).

the right to make a defense to the garnishee 
proceeding. 
In the present case the garnishee 
proceeding succeeded in doing just what the 


Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger 
September 24, 1982  
constitutional provision was created to avoid. 
It harassed the legislator, drove him to make a 
defense in the garnishee proceeding, and 
deprived him of the means of subsistence pending 
the balance of the session. 
We think the case 
clearly comes within the constitutional 
inhibition." 
234 Mich 541-542.

(1976) 395 Mich. 672, 675-676

(3)

Administrative agencies are not courts of justice

Vallejo, 33 Cal. 2d 867 (19 __ ); Caminetti v. Guarantee

Sections 11525, 11180, et~. See People v. Blair,

orders. 
Section 11525.

(1)

(a)

Other jurisdictions: 
(a) 
When legislators have a constitutional

1. 
A grant of immunity "during the session"

(1)

QUESTION: This is an internal FPPC memorandum providing research and legal analysis on legislative immunity from civil process under California constitutional law, rather than a response to a specific legal question.

CONCLUSION: of Research on Legislative Immunity from Civil 
Process and in Civil Proceedings 
I. 
Constitutional Immunity of Legislators from Civil 
Process. 
A. Cal. Const. Art. IV, Section 14: 
A member of the Legislature is not subject to civil 
process during a session of the Legislature or for 
five days before and after a session. 
(Added Nov. 8, 
1966) 
B. The only case interpreting the present section is 
Harmer v. Superior Court, 275 Cal. App. 3d 345 (1969), 
which held: 
(1) 
Art. IV, Section 14 creates an exemption from 
civil process without qualification as to kind or 
subject matter of the lawsuit. 
(2) 
This immunity is designed to benefit the 
public by protecting legislators against compelled 
distraction and interference during the legislative 
session. 
(3) 
The immunity applies to civil process 
generally. 
(4) 
There are conceivable situations where this 
immunity might amount to a denial of due process, but 
this is not one of them. 


Ted, Torn, Bob S., Bob B., Roger 
September 24, 1982  
NOTE: 
A state agency does not have the federal or 
state due process rights of an individual. 
California 
Employment Stablization Comm'n. v. Payne (1947) 31 
C.2d 210, 215. 
C. Definitions of "Civil Process" 
(1) 
Statutory Definitions 
(a) 
Government Code Section 22 -
"Process" 
includes a writ or summons issued in the course of 
judicial proceedings of either a civil or criminal 
nature. 
(b) 
Government Code Section 26660 - Title 3, 
Government of Counties: 
As used in this title: 
(a) 
"Process" includes all writs, warrants, 
summons, and order of courts of justice, or 
judicial officers. 
(b) 
"Notice" includes all papers and orders 
required to be served in any proceedings before 
any court, board, or officer, or when required 
by law to be served independently of such 
proceeding. 
(c) 
Code of Civil Procedure Section 17 
6. 
and the word "process" a writ or

proceedings; 
(2) 
Case Law 
(a) 
"Process is a means whereby a court compels 
a compliance with its demands." 
Woodcourt II 
Limited v. McDonald Company, 119, Cal. App. 3d 245 
(1981); Lister v. Superior Court, 98 Cal. App. 3d 
64 (1979) ; Labrovich v. Georgision, 144 Cal. App. 
2d 567, 574 ( 1976), 5 ° Ca 1. Ju r. 3d., "Pr oc e s s , 
Notice and Subpoenas," Section 1, P. 638. 
Service 
of a summons is civil process. 
Long v. Ansell, 
( 1934) 69 F 2d 386, 


Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger 
September 24, 1982  
(b) 
The definition of "process" has never been 
extended to a proceeding that in no wise emanates 
from or rests upon the authority or jurisdiction of 
a court. 
Meadows v. Bakersfield S. & L. Ass'n., 
259 Cal. App. 2d 749, 753 (1967). 
(Posting notice 
of default and provisions of a deed of trust is not

Caminetti v. Guarantee union Life Ins. 
Co., 22 Cal. 2d 750, 763 (1943). 
(Supersedeas will 
not issue to prevent Ins. Comm'r. from enforcing a 
court-approved agreement; supersedeas will issue 
only to restrain court below or its officers from 
proceeding to enforce a judgment, and it will not 
issue where "no process of or action by the court 
below is involved.") 
(c) 
A subpoena issued by a court clerk or a 
district attorney is not "legal process." 
The 
issuance of such a subpoena is a "purely 
ministerial" act, which cannot be enforced until a 
judicial determiniation of its legality has been 
made. 
People v. Blair (1979) 25 Cal. 3d 640, 648 
(fn 6), 651. 
(d) 
However, the immunity against "civil 
process" has been read broadly by some state 
courts. 
A subpoena duces tecum for a pretrial 
discovery deposition in a judicial proceeding was 
held to be "civil process" for purposes of a 
legislator's constitutional privilege. 
Bishop v. 
Wayne Circuit Judge (1976) 395 Mich. 672, 676. 
So 
was a post-judgment writ of garnishment. 
In 
rejecting the argument that the garnishment, served 
directly 
on the state, was not civil 
process, the court said (quoting an earlier case) : 
"This is to narrow view of the situation. 
The 
idea back of the constitutional provision was to 
protect the legislators from the trouble, worry 
and inconvenience of court proceedings during 
the session, and for a certain time before and 
after, so that the State could have their 
undivided time and attention in public affairs. 
[the legislator], as principal defendant, had 
the right to make a defense to the garnishee 
proceeding. 
In the present case the garnishee 
proceeding succeeded in doing just what the 


Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger 
September 24, 1982  
constitutional provision was created to avoid. 
It harassed the legislator, drove him to make a 
defense in the garnishee proceeding, and 
deprived him of the means of subsistence pending 
the balance of the session. 
We think the case 
clearly comes within the constitutional 
inhibition." 
234 Mich 541-542. 
Bishop v. Wayne Circuit Judge 
(1976) 395 Mich. 672, 675-676 
The Wisconsin Attorney General has also ruled 
that a quasi-garnishment proceeding (which does not 
involve the courts, but only requires a filing with 
the Secretary of State) is civil process for 
purposes of a state constitutional privilege.

Attorney General argued that such a broad 
interpretation was necessary to carry out the 
purposes of the privilege. 
Opinions of the 
Attorney General of the State of Wisconsin, Vol. 
XX, pp. 29-31 (1931). 
See also Auditor General v. 
Wayne Circuit Judge (1926) 234 Mich. 540, 208 NW 
696. 
NOTE: 
A writ of post-judgment garnishment can be 
issued only after a judicial determination of the 
merits of a case. 
(3) 
Is "administrative process" the same as "civil 
process"? 
Administrative agencies are not courts of justice 
within the meaning of the Code of Civil Procedure, and 
administrative agency officials are not officers of 
the court since they derive their power from statute 
and are subject to judicial supervision only to 
prevent arbitrary excercises of power.

Vallejo, 33 Cal. 2d 867 (19 __ ); Caminetti v. Guarantee

Cf., Velkov v. 
Superior Court, 40 Cal. 2d 289 (1953) (State Bar 
disciplinary proceedings are a function of the Supreme 
Court, and State Bar operates as intermediary agency 
in making findings and recommendations). 
Like a 
procecutor's or clerk's subpoena, an administrative 
subpoena can only be enforced after a judicial

September 24, 1982  
determination that it is valid. 
Government Code 
Sections 11525, 11180, et~. See People v. Blair, 
supra. 
The same is true of other administrative 
orders. 
Section 11525. 
Thus an administrative 
agency's orders and subpoenas should not constitute 
"civil process. 11

3. 
A "special session" is also a "session" 
within the constitutional immunity. 
Bishop v. 
Wayne Circuit Judge, supra; Opinions of the 
Attorney General of Wise., Vol XX, 1931, Opinion 
requested by T. Dammann, p. 529, 530. 
4. 
The United States Supreme Court has also 
suggested a liberal construction of the term 
session in a case involving imposition of a 
criminal sentence during a congressional 
adjournment: 
... if ... the privilege ..• extends to an 
arrest for any criillinal offense, such 
privilege would embrace exemption from any 
exertion of power by way of arrest and 
prosecution for the commission of crime, the 
effect of which ... would be to prevent a 
congressman from attending a future as well 
as a pending session of Congress. 
Williamson v. US (1908) 207 US 
425, 434 28 SCt 163, 165. 
(3) 
"Continuous" sessions: 
None of the cases from other jurisdictions 
involved continuous legislative sessions. 
There is 
a suggestion that courts would interpret 
legislative immunity differently in the case of a 
continuous session. 
A question is raised by respondent about the 
possible application of this rule if a 
continuing legislative session is created by 
law. 
We do not decide that question at this 
time. 
However, it has been observed in Harmer 
v. Superior Court, supra, 275 Cal.App.2d at page 
349, 69 Cal.Rptr. at page 857, that "Laws 
creating an immunity from judicial process, 
however temporary, inevitably trench upon the 
judicial function, hence may encounter 
constitutional objections." 
Thurmond v. 
Superior Court, 66 Cal.2d 836, 839-840, 59 Cal.

Rptr. 273, 427 P.2d 985 (1967); Granai v. 
Witters, Longmoore, Akley & Brown, 123 vt. 468, 
194 A.2d 391 (1963). 
Seamans v. Walgren, (1973) 82 
Wash. 2d 771, 514 P.2d 166, 
168. 
E. History of Art. IV, Section 14 
(1) 
Constitution of 1849: 
Art. IV, Section 12 of 
the California Constitution of 1849 provided: 
Members of the Legislature shall, in all cases 
except treason, felony, and breach of the peace, be 
privileged from arrest, and shall not be subject to 
any civil process, during the session of the 
Legislature, nor the fifteen days next before the 
commencement and after the termination of each 
session. 
(2) 
Constitution of 1879: 
In the 1879 
Constitution the section was moved to Article IV, 
Section 11; its text remained unchanged. 
A comparison 
of the 1879 Constitution with those then in effect in 
other states shows numerous similar provisions in 
existence at the time. 
(3) 
1966 Amendment 
In 1966, the California Constitution Revision 
Commission (Feb. 1966) recommended that the section be 
amended as follows: 
Sec. 14. 
A member of the Legislature is not 
subject to civil process during a session of the 
Legislature or for 5 days before and after a 
session. 
Comment: 
Provisions purporting to confer immunity 
from arrest to a legislator were deleted as 
surplusage. 
The courts have determined the 
immunity extends only to civil arrest [In re Emmett 
(1932) 120 Cal. App. 349, 353-357, 7 P. 2d 1096], a 
rarely used device. 


Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger 
September 24, 1982  
The period of immunity from civil process before 
and after legislative sessions was reduced 
om 15 
to 5 days. 
Due to modern modes of travel, the 
legislator no longer needs 15 days to travel to and 
from 
legislative seat. 
The Comment makes it clear that the understanding 
was that this protection covered the actual working 
session of the Legislature and a legislator's travels 
to and from those sessions. 
This is consistent with 
the interpretation given by other states' courts to 
heir re 
tive comparable constitutional or 
statutory protections afforded to members of their 
respective legislative bodies. 
This proposal was adopted in 1966; Section 14 has 
not been amended since. 
( 4) 
Changes in 
sections on legislative 
sessions 
(a) 
When the legislative immunity provision was 
first adopted (1849. the Legislature met only for 
relatively short "sessions." 
The immunity thus d 
not in any way prevent lawsuits against 
legislators. 
(b) 
In 1966, the same year as the last change 
in Article IV, Section 14, the following additional

of 166 days eadh by the California Constitution

II. 
During the two-year term for which 
Assemblymen are elected, the Legislature will 
be a continuous body. 
Legislation remaining 
at the end of the first session will survive 
until the end of the following year's 
session .•.• 
(c) 
1972 Amendment. 
A new proposal was adopted 
in 1972, which changed the sessions from 166 days 
per year to continuous sessions. 
There was no 
corresponding change in Article IV, Sec. 14. 
ballot argument in favor or Proposition 4 (November 
1972) which created the two-year sessions contained 
the following assertions: 
The amendment does not mean that the 
Legislature will be meeting continuously in 
Sacramento for two years. 
In fact, there are 
more limits on the amount of time the 
Legislature may act on Legislation than 
currently exist. 
These limits are designed to 
give the 
islature a more 
siness-like set of 
deadlines for its operation. 
The amendment will not result in any increase 
in compensation or benefits to legislators. 
There is no indication that anyone considered 
the 
ssible effect of this amendment on Art. IV, 
Section 14, or that anyone intended to provide 
legislators with immunity from civil process during 
virtually their entire term of office. 
(d) 
Present Legislative practice: 
The 
Legislature now convenes on the first Monday in 
December of even-numbered years and "adjourns sine 
die" on November 30 two years later, it "adjourns" 
in "final recess" on the 31st of August in even 
numbered years. 
In addition, there are various 
other "adjournments" and recesses which occur 
during 
course of the "continuous" two-year

A. 
Code of 
il Pr 
ure Section 595 prov 
s: 


Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger 
September 24, 1982  
The trial of any civil action, or proceeding in a 
court, or of any administrative proceeding before a 
state board or commission or officer, irrespective of 
the date of the filing thereof or when it became at 
issue, or the hearing of any motion, demurrer, or 
other proceeding, shall be postponed to a date certain 
when it appers to the court, board, commission, or 
officer before which such action or proceeding is 
pending hat either a party thereto, or any attorney of 
record therein (whether he became an attorney of 
record before or after the commencement of a 
legislative session or before or after his appointment 
to a legislative committee), or a principal witness, 
is a Member of the Legislature of this state and that 
the Legislature is in session or recess (not exceeding 
a recess of forty (40) days •••• 
When the Legislature is in session or in recess such 
action or proceeding shall not, without the consent of 
the attorney of record therein, be brought on for 
trial or hearing before the expiration of thirty (30) 
days next following final adjournment of the 
Legislature or the commencement of a recess of more 
than forty (40) days. 
If a date is available during 
recess, continuance shall be given if possible to such 
earlier date •••• 
Granting of a continuance pursuant to this section is 
mandatory unless the court determines that such 
continuance would defeat or abridge a right to relief 
pendente lite in a paternity action or a right to 
invoke a provisional remedy such as pendente lite 
support in a domestic relations controversy, 
attachment and sale of pershiable goods, receivership 
of a failing business, and temporary restraining order 
or preliminary injunction, and that the continuance 
should not be granted •••• 
B. 
Case Law (California) 
(1) 
836: 
Thurmond v. Superior Court (9167) 66 Cal. 2d 


Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger 
September 24, 1982  
(a) 
The Legislature may not enact laws 
concerning continuances which unduly interfere with 
the court's inherent (constitutional) power to 
control its casework and to safeguard the rights of 
litigants. 
Any such law will be read as directory 
(not mandatory) . 
(After Thurmond Section 595 was 
amended to state explicity that it is mandatory.) 
(b) 
When a right to relief pendente lite (in 
this case for support for an unborn child) would be 
materially impaired or destroyed by allowing 
continuances under Section 595, the court in its 
discretion should not apply Section 595. 
This may 
also apply to other provisional remedies in cases 
involving "substantial existing rights" which could 
be "defeated or abridged by extended continuances" 
Id. at 839-840. 
(2) 
Beckley v. Reclamation Bd., 48 Cal. 2d 710 
(1957). 
A lawyer-legislator is entitled to continuances 
under Section 595 while he is pursuing his 
legislative duties; it is an abuse of discretion 
for a trial court to not vacate a judgment when 
such continuances are not granted. 
(3) 
Oil Workers Intern. Union, CIO v. Sup. Ct., 
103 Cal. App. 2d 52 (1951). 
Section 595 does not apply before the Session 
begins; it only applies when the Legislature is 
actually in Session or in recess during a Session. 
(4) 
Peo. v. Goldenson, 76 Cal. 328 (1888). 
(a) 
Section 595 applies only to those who are 
attorneys of record before the Session begins. 
(b) 
The objectives of Section 595 are to 
protect clients whose attorneys were called to 
their legislative duties and to secure lawyers in 
the Legislature from loss to their private 
business. 


Ted, Tom, Bo b S., Bo b B., Rog e r 
September 24, 1982  
C. Ca se Law (0 ther j ur i sd ictions) : 
Other states have held unconstitutional statutes 
similar to Section 595 which granted legislators a 
right to continuances or a privilege against civil 
process. 
(1) 
A Vermont statute similar to Section 595 
granted an attorney-legislator a privilege against 
having his cases heard during a legislative session 
unless the legislator waived the privilege. 
The 
Vermont court held this unconstitutional because it 
violated the state constitution's grant of judicial 
powers to the courts (separation of powers., and 
because it violated a state constitutional 
prohibition against granting "special privileges" 
to legislators (or others., Granai v. Witters, 
Longmoore, Akley & Brown (1963) A2d 391. 
(2) 
An Illinois statute provided that 
legislators were immune from civil process during 
the session of the Legislature. 
This provision was 
held to violate a state constitutional prohibition 
(like Vermont's) against any "special laws" which 
grant any individual "any special or exclusive 
privilege [or] immunity." 
"The members of the 
General Assembly constitute but a portion of the 
public officials of the state, and we perceive no 
good reason why they should be singled out as 
immune from service of civil process while engaged 
in the line of their duty any more than the members 
of any other class of public official." 
Phillips 
v. Browne, 270 Ill. 450, 110 N.E. 601, 602 (1915). 
(3) 
Other jurisdictions have also held similar 
statutes to be an unconstitutional infringement of 
judicial powers. 
Lemoine v. Martineau (R.I. 1975) 
342 A2d 616, 621; City v. Valdez Development Co. 
(Alas. 1973) 506 P.2d 1279; Williams v. Borden's, 
262 SE 2d 881. 
III. 
Other immunities or privileges from judicial process. 
A. Immunity rule for nonresident witnesses and attorneys 
was founded on the convenience of the court, and this 
privilege from service always ceased when the benefit 


Ted, Tom, B9b S., Bob B., Roger 
September 24, 1982  
to the court ended. 
Gaines v. Superior Court, 196 
Cal. App. 2d 749 (1961) (application to nonresident 
attorneys); People v. Wilson, 213 Cal. App. 2d 290 
(1963); Velkor v. Superior Court, supra. 
This rule was essentially abolished in California 
by the Court of Appeal and a liberalized service of 
process statute. 
Severn v. Adidas Sportschuhfabriken, 
33 Cal. App. 3d 754 (1976) (outlines history of 
immuni ty rule). 
CCP Section 410.10 et ~.; 50 Cal. 
Jur. 3d "Process, Notice, and Subpoenas," Section 53 
pp. 701- 703. 
B. Members of the armed forces will not normally be 
exempt from civil process under California law. 
Murrey v. Murrey, 216 Cal. 707 (1932); Tulley v. 
Superior Court, 45 Cal. App. 2d 24 (1941); 50 Cal. 
Jur. 3d, "Process, Notice and Subpoenas," Section 55, 
p. 704. 
In both of these cases, the courts did not grant an 
exemption to service. 
The courts recognized the 
public policy behind such an exemption for all 
nonresidents within the jurisdiction engaged in the 
performance of a public duty, but they rejected its 
application under the particular facts they were faced 
with. 
The Tully court noted that the trial court, 
upon a proper showing, could grant reasonable 
continuances and otherwise protect the defendant's 
rights to prepare his case. 
C. Foreign diplomatic officials are immune from process 
of state courts based on the doctrine of sovereign 
immunity from suit. 
Federal courts have exclusive 
jurisdiction of all actions and proceedings against 
foreign officials by statute. 
U.S. Const., Art. III, 
Section 2. 
22 U.S.C. Section 252 and 28 u.s.c. 
Sections 1251 and 1351; 50 Cal. Jur. 3d "Process, 
Notice and Subpoenas," Section 56, p. 705. 
State courts have jurisdiction over a suit against 
a consular representative and citizen of a foreigh 
country for divorce and alimony since federal courts 
have no jurisdiction over divorce. 
The term "suits 
against consuls and vice-consuls" in the statute must 


Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger 
September 24, 1982  
be taken to refer to ordinary civil proceedings and 
not divorce proceedings. 
State of Ohio ex reI. 
Popovici, 50 S.Ct. 154 (1930). 
NOTE: 
None of the above immunities are based on a 
C'Oi1Stitutionally conferred privilege. 
Accordingly, the 
discretion and flexibility allowed in the application of 
these immunities are not necessarily available to the 
courts in determining the extent of a legislator's 
immunity under Art. IV, Sec. 14. 
However, they support 
the court's ability to interpret Section 595 narrowly, 
and also provide some support for the argument that the 
constitutional immunity from civil process should be 
construed narrowly and literally. 
See also Long v. 
Ansell, 55 S.Ct. 21 (1934), where the Supreme Court held 
that a U.S. Senator's constitutional privilege against 
civil arrest did not extend to an immunity from civil 
process. 
"Its language is exact and leaves no room for a 
construction which would extend the privilege beyond the 
terms of the grant." 
Id., at 22. 
But see cases cited in 
Section I C (2) (d), above.

embedding.first_500_words: State of California Memorandum M- 82-163 DATE: September 24, 1982 TO: Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger FROM: Fair Political/Practices Commission Barbara Milman/[illegible] Fishburn, Bob Leigbh RE: Summary of Research on Legislative Immunity from Civil Process and in Civil Proceedings I. Constitutional Immunity of Legislators from Civil Process. A. Cal. Const. Art. IV, Section 14: A member of the legislature is not subject to civil process during a session of the legislature or for five days before and after a session. (Added Nov. 8, 1966) B. The only case interpreting the present section is Harmer v. Superior Court, 275 Cal. App. 3d 345 (1969), which held: (1) Art. IV, Section 14 creates an exemption from civil process without qualification as to kind or subject matter of the lawsuit. (2) This immunity is designed to benefit the public by protecting legislators against compelled distraction and interference during the legislative session. (3) The immunity applies to civil process generally. (4) There are conceivable situations where this immunity might amount to a denial of due process, but this is not one of them. Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger September 24, 1982 Page 2 NOTE: A state agency does not have the federal or state due process rights of an individual. California Employment Stabilization Comm'n. v. Payne (1947) 31 C.2d 210, 215. C. Definitions of "Civil Process" (1) Statutory Definitions (a) Government Code Section 22 - "Process" includes a writ or summons issued in the course of judicial proceedings of either a civil or criminal nature. (b) Government Code Section 26660 - Title 3, Government of Counties: As used in this title: (a) "Process" includes all writs, warrants, summons, and order of courts of justice, or judicial officers. (b) "Notice" includes all papers and orders required to be served in any proceedings before any court, board, or officer, or when required by law to be served independently of such proceeding. (c) Code of Civil Procedure Section 17 6. ... and the word "process" a writ or summons issued in the course of judicial proceedings; (2) Case Law (a) "Process is a means whereby a court compels a compliance with its demands." Woodcourt II Limited v. McDonald Company, 119, Cal. App. 3d 245 (1981); Lister v. Superior Court, 98 Cal. App. 3d 64 (1979); Labrovich v. Georgision, 144 Cal. App. 2d 567, 574 (1976), 50 Cal. Jur. 3d., "Process, Notice and Subpoenas," Section 1, P. 638. Service of a summons is civil process. Long v. Ansell, (1934) 69 F2d 386, ___. Ted, Tom, Bob S., Bob B., Roger September 24, 1982 Page 3 (b) The definition of "process" has never been extended to a proceeding that in no wise emanates from or rests upon the authority or jurisdiction of a court. Meadows v. Bakersfield S. & L. Ass'n., 259 Cal. App. 2d 749, 753 (1967). (Posting notice of default and provisions of a deed of trust is not "process.") Caminetti v. Guarantee Union Life Ins. Co., 22 Cal. 2d 750, 763 (1943).
